name: einops (Flexible Tensor Operations)

performance.notes: Near-zero overhead; compiles to native framework ops

agent_readiness.notes: Einstein-inspired notation for tensor operations. rearrange(x, 'b c h w -> b (c h w)'). reduce(x, 'b c h w -> b c', 'mean'). repeat(x, 'b c -> b c h w', h=4, w=4). Works with NumPy, PyTorch, TensorFlow, JAX, Cupy. Named dimensions for clarity. Replaces confusing transpose/reshape/view chains. Pack/unpack for variable-length sequences. asnumpy() for testing. Used in transformers, diffusion models, vision models. Alex Rogozhnikov (Yandex). Massively reduces tensor manipulation bugs.

gotchas: Requires learning Einstein notation (but pays off quickly)
No dynamic shapes support in rearrange (shapes must be known)
Debugging errors requires understanding the notation
best_when: Deep learning code with complex tensor reshaping (transformers, CNNs)
Any PyTorch/TensorFlow/JAX code with messy view/reshape/permute chains
Research code where tensor shape bugs are common
summary: einops provides readable tensor operations via Einstein notation. rearrange(x, 'b c h w -> b (c h w)'), reduce(x, '... h w -> ...', 'mean'), repeat. Works with NumPy, PyTorch, JAX, TensorFlow. Self-documenting tensor shapes prevent bugs. Used in transformer and diffusion model implementations. Replaces confusing view/reshape/permute chains. Essential for readable ML research code.
